title: The Disproportionate Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (7)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Obvious OCR intrusions and broken word or numeral joins have been repaired. The source's printed figures are preserved even where stated totals do not agree arithmetically with the listed amounts, including the Secunderabad deposits and the four land purchases registered in Sasikala's name. The source also prints Rs. 21 as the salary for the first twenty-one months, and that wording is retained. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Here are further details from the testimony compiled and presented by government counsel Bhavani Singh in the Jayalalithaa disproportionate-assets case being heard in Bengaluru:

In his testimony, Sivasankar stated: “I live in Rasipuram in Salem district. My mother Lakshmiammal owned 3.84 acres of land at Nannil in Tiruvarur district. Invoking Sasikala’s name, they purchased that land for Rs. 3.84 lakh and registered it in the name of Ramaraj Agro Mill.”
Saminathan testified that his 3.30 acres at Paiyanur on East Coast Road had been purchased for Rs. 1.65 lakh and registered in the name of V.N. Sudhakaran.

Govindarajan, a manager at SIDCO, testified that an application had been made for the allotment of 1.12 acres to Anjaneya Printers. Sulochana Sampath, then chairperson of SIDCO, considered it and recommended that the land be allotted. Accepting that recommendation, Pandey, IAS, then SIDCO’s managing director, allotted the land for Rs. 8,59,590.

An application was also made seeking three grounds of SIDCO land for Super Duper TV. Under the rules, any application for land had to be examined by a three-member committee, and an allotment could be made only if that committee recommended it. SIDCO manager Chandran testified, however, that the rules were violated and the land was allotted to the television company on the very day the application was submitted.

Bhavani, who had served as manager of Indian Bank’s Abhiramapuram branch in Chennai, testified that Jaya Publications, in which the first accused Jayalalithaa was a partner, received a bank loan of Rs. 1.50 crore in 1992. It was from this money that “Amrutanjan Mansion,” a well-known property at Luz Corner in Mylapore, was bought. The land belonged to Ramayi Ammal, while the building belonged to her brother Nageswara Rao. Nageswara Rao had mortgaged the building and borrowed Rs. 1.10 crore. That loan had been obtained from the same bank in which Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Ilavarasi held accounts. After learning of it through bank officials, Jayalalithaa’s side immediately inspected the mansion, paid the bank only the outstanding loan amount, and, through threats and compulsion, bought the property. Nageswara Rao had been arranging to sell it to another buyer for Rs. 2.5 crore. Jayalalithaa’s side prevented that transaction and intimidated him into selling at a lower price. A bank officer named Ramesh confirmed in his own testimony the details Nageswara Rao had given.

Paramark testified that Jayalalithaa deposited Rs. 6.30 lakh on 13-1-1993, Rs. 8.50 lakh on 29-1-1993, Rs. 6.20 lakh on 8-1-1994 and Rs. 9 lakh on 24-12-1994 in a bank at Secunderabad in Andhra Pradesh, described in the source as deposits totalling Rs. 25 lakh.

Jayaraman, the village administrative officer of Mannargudi in Thanjavur district, testified that 9.89 acres had been purchased and registered in Sasikala’s name, with Natarajan identified in the document as her husband, and that 13.57 acres had been bought from three people in her name. Mohammed Ayub testified that 13.57 acres belonging to three people at Tiruthuraipoondi in Tiruvarur district had been purchased and registered in Sasikala’s name. The government counsel also placed before the court statistics showing how much land had been registered in whose name, and stated that properties bought in Tamil Nadu were registered not by going to the registration offices, but by having the relevant sub-registrars summoned to the home of the first accused, Jayalalithaa, where the documents were registered.

Riverway Agro alone acquired 948.80 acres, worth several crores of rupees, through more than one hundred registered deeds, and the land was registered in the name of V.N. Sudhakaran, the third accused in the case.

Land at Paiyanur near Chennai belonging to music composer Gangai Amaran and his wife Manimekalai was bought for Rs. 9.80 lakh. Bhavani Singh explained that it had been bought at a throwaway price, far below the market value of the day, and registered in the name of Sasikala, the second accused in the case.

The government counsel said that land belonging to Ramaiah was bought for Rs. 1 lakh, land belonging to Kandhabai for Rs. 3,04,500, land belonging to Vasanthabai for Rs. 3,46,500, and land belonging to Ashok Kumar for Rs. 2.84 lakh, with the source describing the total paid for these lands registered in Sasikala’s name as Rs. 17.26 lakh.

Bhavani Singh supplied figures showing that, at Siruthavur, 11.8 acres belonging to Amanullah were purchased for Rs. 1.90 lakh; 10.86 acres belonging to Gopinath for Rs. 1.80 lakh; another 7.44 acres belonging to Amanullah for Rs. 1.10 lakh; and 3.03 acres belonging to Muniyan for Rs. 1.07 lakh. Three buildings belonging to Palanisamy, each measuring 3,197 square feet, were registered after payment of Rs. 15 lakh.
For Meadow Agro Farm, 113.21 acres were purchased from several people and registered for Rs. 16,94,250.

For Ramaraj Agro, 18.58 acres belonging to Gandhi were purchased and registered for Rs. 3.96 lakh; 3.84 acres belonging to Lakshmi were purchased for Rs. 76,800; and 8.01 acres belonging to Asokan for Rs. 1,62,200. A 6,943-square-foot building belonging to Wade Ramesh was purchased and registered for Rs. 90 lakh for Anjaneya Printers. Bhavani Singh also stated that a building valued at Rs. 26 lakh had been bought and registered for Lex Properties.

In his testimony, Rajagopal, Inspector General of Tamil Nadu’s Registration Department, noted that land and buildings had been accumulated in the names of the accused Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi across many parts of Chennai, including Injambakkam, Vettuvankeni, Neelankarai, Paiyanur, Triplicane and Guindy, and in many other places including Tirunelveli, Thanjavur, Kodaikanal, Kodanadu and Andhra Pradesh.

Rajagopal testified that a real-estate proprietor named Siva first obtained a general power of attorney in his own name over 1,190 acres belonging to farmers in several Kanyakumari villages, including Meenankulam, Sivarankulam and Vellankulam, and then conveyed those lands in the name of Riverway Agro Farm at a sub-registrar’s office in Chennai. The owners of Riverway Agro Farm were Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi.

He further testified that he had been asked to register these lands at an undervaluation and with reduced stamp duty. When he refused, a sub-registrar who had come from Chennai insisted that he should register them without objection because it was a matter involving those at the top. Rajagopal still refused. The registration was therefore not done there; power-of-attorney documents were prepared and registered at the Chennai office that was under Rajagopal’s charge.

While presenting the testimony, government counsel Bhavani Singh made only a passing reference to the purchase, in the name of the “Sandhya Natya Kala Niketan” trust bearing her mother’s name, of a building beside the Poes Garden residence. He did not state that Jayalalithaa was the sole trustee of the trust. But Judge Cunha himself asked, “Was Jayalalithaa not the sole trustee of that trust, and was that not why she signed and bought the property?” Bhavani Singh replied, “Yes.”

The 898-acre Kodanadu tea estate at Ooty had belonged to an Englishman, Craig Jones, who attempted to sell it. Two major companies, Addisons and Essar, came forward to buy it. Once this became known, a group arrived in a car without a number plate, threatened the estate manager and said that the Kodanadu estate must be sold only to a particular family from Chennai. Craig Jones lodged a police complaint on 23-10-1995. The very next day, the district superintendent of police allegedly intimidated him into withdrawing it. The tea estate, then worth Rs. 10 crore, was registered in the name of the Udaiyar family for Rs. 7.5 crore. Within a month or two, however, it was registered in the names of Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi. Bhavani Singh placed these details from the testimony before the court.
Bhavani Singh also cited Ganapathi Pillai’s testimony that he had conveyed the house in which he lived at V.O.C. Nagar in Thanjavur city to V.N. Sudhakaran for Rs. 11 lakh.

A modern bus was bought through a private company for Rs. 10 lakh, and the task of adding further amenities was entrusted to a bus-body builder in Mumbai. Gurudev Singh of Mumbai testified that Rs. 23 lakh was spent converting it into a luxury bus, fitted with two air-conditioning units, a Sony television, a VCR, an American toilet, granite-marble flooring and teakwood fittings. He said the modernisation was carried out under the supervision of the person then serving as Tamil Nadu’s transport minister, and that the finished bus was taken to Jayalalithaa’s Poes Garden residence, where Jayalalithaa and Sasikala inspected and approved it. Bhavani Singh explained with supporting evidence that the luxury bus was registered in the name of Jaya Publications.

Bhavani Singh stated that a chemical factory on 3.5 acres at Cuddalore had been bought for Rs. 30 lakh, and land in the same area belonging to James Patrick for Rs. 50 lakh, with both registered in the names of companies operated by the accused. A further three acres nearby were also purchased.

He also cited testimony concerning V.N. Sudhakaran’s purchase and registration, for Rs. 90 lakh, of four grounds of land belonging to Tamil film producer Ramesh in Chennai’s T. Nagar. A Canara Bank officer testified that Jayalalithaa, the first accused, deposited Rs. 1 crore in 1992 for a three-year term, while Sasikala, the second accused, deposited Rs. 25 lakh.

A government officer responsible for company registrations gave detailed statistical evidence about the dates on which Jaya Publications, Sasi Enterprises, Lex Properties, Riverway Agro Farm, Signora Business, Meadow Agro Farm, Bean Beach, Ramaraj Agro Farm, Super Duper TV, Indo Chemicals and Anjaneya Printers were registered; in whose names; who their chairpersons, managing directors and shareholders were; and what businesses they conducted. Bhavani Singh also placed before the court testimony from a senior Tamil Nadu IAS officer, Sheila Balakrishnan, and testimony that all the lands acquired from farmers were registered in the names of companies whose shareholders included the accused in this case. Witnesses stated that 426.62 acres obtained from several people were registered in the name of Riverway Agro on 21 and 22 December 1992.

Bhavani Singh told the Bengaluru Special Court that during Jayalalithaa’s five years as Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, she drew a salary of one rupee a month only for the first twenty-one months, amounting to Rs. 21, yet had constructed buildings worth Rs. 14 crore. He further said that a search of her residence yielded 23 kilograms of gold and diamond jewellery valued at Rs. 90 lakh.

In his testimony, Vasudevan, appointed by the Tamil Nadu government to value the gold and diamond jewellery seized from the residences of Jayalalithaa and Sasikala, the accused in the disproportionate-assets case, stated that 23 kilograms and 113 grams of gold jewellery were seized from No. 36, Poes Garden. He valued the gold at Rs. 91,57,253 and the diamond jewellery at Rs. 2,43,92,790, for a combined total of Rs. 3,35,50,043.
Vasudevan further testified that at No. 31-A, Poes Garden, he valued gold jewellery worth Rs. 47,61,816 and 1,116 silver articles worth Rs. 55,80,000.

He also gave detailed figures for the weight and value of thirteen categories of articles seized from Giri Shanmugam’s house on Chevalier Sivaji Ganesan Road, whose total value was Rs. 11,94,382. Are these the only pieces of testimony brought out by government counsel Bhavani Singh? (To be continued.)
With affection,
M.K.
